text: The surgeon opens the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon medially, facilitating dissection from the body and tail of the pancreas due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery. The peritoneal leaf of the left mesocolon is etched caudally, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon performs vascular dissection, revealing the left colic vein, which is separated from the left colic artery and sealed with a radiofrequency device at its confluence with the anterior mesenteric vein. The left colic artery is then sectioned. The peritoneal leaf of the mesosigma and proximal rectum is etched from cranial to caudal. Vascular dissection begins posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery and proceeds along the inferior mesenteric vein, which is divided from the left colic artery running behind.

During the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery, the hypogastric plexus is preserved. The surgeon reveals the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta and the bifurcation of the left colic artery from the main pedicle. The left colic vein is visible descending from the splenic flexure to its confluence with the inferior mesenteric vein. The inferior mesenteric vein, superior rectal artery, and sigmoid artery are sectioned using the low-tie technique without clips or suture devices.
